item.title: Forza Horizon 6 PC players urged to check their Microsoft Gaming Services version, as Playground tackle disappearing saves
item.content:encoded:
Following some instances of Forza Horizon 6 players losing saves and ending up having to start over from scratch, developers Playground Games are starting to roll out fixes. For PC folks, they're advising a check that the latest version of Microsoft Gaming Services is running, as the app is the way PC-centric fixes for the issue are being rolled out.

"Our team is investigating an issue impacting some Forza Horizon 6 players where game save progress may be lost or reset," Playground's support team wrote in a notice. The advised any affected players to open a ticket on the Forza Support website the same day the save's lost and playing the game or creating any new saves until the support team have looked into the case.

For those of us playing on Windows PC via either the MS Store or Steam fortunate enough not to have lost a garage worth of customised Peel P60s, the advice of writing is to make sure that your version of the Microsoft Gaming Services app is running version 37.114.10001.0 or higher. You can check that by heading to your system settings, then apps and installed apps. Search through the installed apps for Gaming Services, then click the ellipsis and open up advanced options. In there will be a big long version number like the one earlier in this paragraph. If it's not up to date, you'll need to set that in motion by opening the downloads section of the Microsoft Store, checking for updates, and setting Gaming Services away updating.

Beyond that, take care to make sure you exit the game properly via the menu, rather than ducking out mid-loading screen for example, and give the game "at least a few minutes" to sync progress with the cloud after exiting before you then knock your PC off. Maybe set up a mandatory five minutes of trying to doodle whichever hatchback you've just slapped an anime girl-heavy livery on, just to make sure the save mechanisms have had time to do their thing.

If you're still wondering whether to give Horizon 6 a go, and the BDS movement's ongoing boycott of Microsoft over the company's links to the Israeli military makes not doing so totally understandable, here's my Forza Horizon 6 review.



https://www.rockpapershotgun.com/forza-horizon-6-pc-players-urged-to-check-their-microsoft-gaming-services-version-as-playground-tackle-disappearing-saves

item.title: Brazilian Woman Wins $500,000 Playing Candy Crush Saga
item.content:encoded:
The Candy Crush All Stars tournament has wrapped, and standing at the top of the table over millions of competitors stands a Brazilian woman named Luana. At the live final in London, ten contestants took to the stage to fight for the lion’s share of a massive $1 million by playing what might seem like one of the unlikeliest games imaginable.

Luana, the champion from Bahia, Brazil, walks away with a $500,000 cut and a one-of-a-kind championship ring, adorned with sapphires, rubies, and emeralds.

Mobile Gaming Wins Again

Candy Crush is often dismissed as being as casual a game as you could think of, but it just won an unassuming player from Brazil a whopping $500,000. The Candy Crush All Stars tournament represents the pinnacle of the esports scene in the space and drew the attention of thousands of viewers.

In a statement, Luana explained:

I’ve been playing for as long as I can remember. I used to be really imaginative. I’ve dreamt about competing in live tournaments, picture a host commentating on my moves, imagine the venue, the opponents.

The fact that ‘Candy Crush All Stars’ exists and I got to compete in it feels like it came straight out of my own imagination. I feel like I’m honouring a younger version of myself by literally living her dreams.

You can check out Luana’s winning moment here:

Mobile esports are immensely powerful, pulling in millions of fans with each successive event. It’s no big secret that mobile gaming makes up the most densely populated and valuable vertical in the industry, but the mobile esports space is also growing at a staggering pace.

Tournaments like the Candy Crush All Stars are a testament to that fact.

Do you think you’re good enough at Candy Crush to win a $500,000 prize? Let us know your thoughts on the Insider Gaming Discord server.

For more Insider Gaming coverage, check out the news that Blizzard has revealed Shion’s new abilities

Senior Editor and Esports Lead

Grant has been gaming for 30+ years and in the industry for 10+. You'll probably find him playing a post-apocalyptic game or an extraction shooter somewhere.

More from Grant Taylor-Hill

item.title: Microsoft Reportedly Used Activision-Blizzard to Subsidize XBOX
item.content:encoded:
A new report offers some insight into how Microsoft planned to use Call of Duty to offset declining revenue from XBOX Studios. However, it backfired when Call of Duty: Black Ops 7 led to an off year for the franchise.

XBOX is in the midst of major changes in 2026. The shuffling of the executive structure, which led to Asha Sharma becoming CEO of the brand, has resulted in some positive changes and momentum. Still, tough decisions must be made, and it was recently revealed that mass layoffs are planned for July 2026. Now, a new report examines some of the dominoes that fell for Microsoft’s gaming division to get to this point.

Call of Duty Sales Decline Added to XBOX’s Challenges

A report from Jez Corden of Windows Central notes that Microsoft had been using profits from Activision-Blizzard to subsidize the XBOX brand, but after Call of Duty had an off year, it added another issue to the pile XBOX faced.

“Putting Call of Duty into XBOX Game Pass cannibalized both sides. As players fled the $30 price tag, there was less money for XBOX Game Pass to subsidize Call of Duty’s cannibalized sales for those who remained. The model broke under its own weight and had to be reset back to its lower price, wiping out a year or more of growth,” the report notes. “With XBOX unable to acquire hardware to sell to find new members in XBOX Game Pass, which has been the pot of money XBOX has been using to subsidize other parts of the business, once again, the model began to break down.”

Moving forward, XBOX has already begun adjusting its approach. Games like Gears of War: E-Day will be console exclusives moving forward. However, the top-tier games will also be playable on its Xbox Game Pass. In his report, Corden considers what could be done to help reverse course at XBOX. One such pitch involves removing some of the Heavy Hitters from the Game Pass service to potentially increase profits from Standalone sales. The example he uses is Forza Horizon.

He also suggests adding another premium tier to the service where you pay a bit more, but you can pick some premium games as part of your subscription, such as Call of Duty or an added-on World of Warcraft subscription.

In the past, it was reported that an ad-supported Game Pass tier was considered a cheaper entry-level option for players. It’s also been considered that Netflix could be bundled in with a Game Pass subscription. Learn more about that here.

Another major issue XBOX is facing, of course, is the rising price of memory components. Recently, XBOX CSO Matthew Ball noted that the company was reassessing its approach to the next-generation console, Project Helix. Learn more about that here.

Sound off about this story and all others in our official Discord server. Also, don’t forget to sign up for our weekly newsletter.

item.title: Pour One Out For The D&D-Inspired Visual Novel That Rolled An Unfortunate Direct-Day Launch
item.content:encoded:
Let's (Tavern) Talk About It

We've seen game releases doing everything they can to get out the way of GTA 6's November launch window, but what do you do when a major gaming event is announced with a mere 24-hour notice? There's no way to avoid that unfortunate timing.

That's the misfortune that befell Gentle Troll and the launch of its visual novel prequel, Tavern Talk Stories: Dreamwalker. The game launched earlier this week on 9th June — a day that, thanks to the Direct, left most of us distracted by the likes of Ocarina of Time and Kingdom Hearts IV — so we thought it was only fair to let it have a roll at the limelight again.

Much like 2024's Tavern Talk, this D&D-inspired visual novel is all about mixing up drinks for your fantastical bar's patrons, learning their likes and dislikes, and potentially altering their quests along the way. While set in the same world as its predecessor, Dreamwalker takes place 34 years in the past, opening room for a whole new cast of characters in need of a tipple.

Here's a rundown of the game's key features and a handful of screens:

- The Drowsy Dragon is home to a colourful cast of characters inspired by TTRPGs. Get to know their unique personalities, and help decide where their story goes.
- A new story within Asteria, set 36 years before the events of Tavern Talk
- Serve a menu of magical, fate-altering drinks to your patrons, but choose carefully, as there’s more than one solution!
- Gather rumours from the tavern-goers and use them to create quests for your adventurous guests.
- Have fun with a refinement of the drink mixing and quest-making mechanics to have a dash of a challenge.
- Discover 3 unique endings as you attempt to stop dreams from twisting into nightmares!
- Immersive reading experience of at least 10 hours on your first play-through.

The game is now available on the Switch eShop for £13.99 / $15.99 (with a 10% discount added for the launch period), or as a Bundle with Tavern Talk for £29.50 / $33.99.

Will this one be rolling its way onto your wishlist? Let us know in the comments.

Jim came to Nintendo Life in 2022 and, despite his insistence that The Minish Cap is the best Zelda game and his unwavering love for the Star Wars prequels (yes, really), he has continued to write news and features on the site ever since.

item.title: Alkimia Interactive’s Gothic 1 Remake Cuts Through the Summer Game Fest Craze, Hits 500K Copies Sold in First Week
item.content:encoded:
The recently released Gothic 1 Remake from developer Alkimia Interactive and publisher THQ Nordic has officially reached 500,000 copies sold in just its first week on digital store shelves, the studio announced. It's a significant milestone not just because of its scale, but because it's done so during one of the nosiest months in the video game industry, Summer Game Fest 2026.

This remake has been a long time coming, and the original Gothic was such an influential release when it first arrived all the way back in 2001, which gives it a leg up on an RPG like it that was just a brand-new franchise. But that doesn't make it any less significant that it has been able to find an audience right now, at a time when the biggest games in the industry are the ones that haven't even arrived yet.

There are undoubtedly plenty of players whose focus is more on games like God of War Laufey, The Legend of Zelda: Ocarina of Time Remake, and Resident Evil: Veronica, the top-three most engaged with and discussed games from this past week's bonanza of news.

THQ Nordic and Alkimia Interactive even note the engagement the game has gotten beyond checkout, with a peak of nearly 78K concurrent players on Steam the weekend of its launch, and a 'Very Positive' user reviews rating.

"Alkimia Interactive and THQ Nordic are thrilled by this initial success and extend a heartfelt thank you to all players who have entered the Colony so far," the game's Steam page reads. "Especially to all of the long-time dedicated fans who have supported the Gothic franchise over the past 25 years. This milestone would not have been possible without such a passionate community."

The statement adds that Alkimia will "continue to support and improve the Gothic 1 Remake in the weeks and months ahead."

For more on the Gothic 1 Remake, check out our interview with director Kai Rosenkran, Alkimia Interactive studio head Reinhard Pollice, and community manager Timur Khodyrev, along with our own hands-on impressions.

About the author: David has been writing about videogames, technology, and culture since 2020, with a focus on reporting daily news across multiple publications, including GameDaily.Biz, GameSkinny, and PlayStation Universe before joining Wccftech in 2025. David started contributing as Canada/US reporter for Wccftech's gaming section in 2025. Besides being up-to-date on the industry's movements, he loves interviewing developers, reviewing games, and writing intricate essays about the symbolism and layered meanings to be found in rich narratives as he's done for publications like GamesIndustry.Biz, LostInCult, and others. Outside of games he loves movies, music, theatre, his hometown, and his family, though not necessarily in that order.

Follow Wccftech on Google to get more of our news coverage in your feeds.
